coredata.dc:title: MXene nanosheet-derived N, S-codoped graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive and selective detection of 3-nitro- l -tyrosine in human serum
coredata.dc:creator: Anh, Nguyen Thi Ngoc
Huynh, Trung Viet
Nguyen, Van Thanh
Nguyen, Thi Kim Anh
Doong, Ruey-an
coredata.dc:description: 3-Nitro-l-tyrosine (3NT) is an oxidative stress metabolite associated with neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease and rheumatoid arthritis. In this study, the N, S-co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQDs) derived from nitrogen-doped Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheet via the hydrothermal method in the presence of mercaptosuccinic acid was synthesized as an optical sensing probe to detect 3NT in human serum. Tetramethyl ammonium hydroxide, the nitrogen source and delamination agent, was used to prepare nitrogen-doped MXene nanosheets via one step at room temperature. The as-prepared NSGQDs are uniform with an average size of 1.2 ± 0.6 nm, and can be stable in aqueous solution for at least 90 d to serve as the fluorescence probe. The N atoms in N-MXene reduce the restacking and aggregation of MXene nanosheets, while the sulfur dopant in NSGQDs increases the quantum yield from 6.2 to 12.1 % as well as enhances the selectivity of 3NT over the other 12 interferences via coordination interaction with nitro group in 3NT. A linear range of 0.02–150 μM in PBS and 0.05–200 μM in human serum with a recovery of 97–108 % for 3NT detection is observed. Moreover, the limit of detection can be lowered to 4.2 and 7 nM in PBS and 1 × diluted human serum, respectively. Results obtained clearly indicate the potential application of the N–Ti3C2Tx derived NSGQD for effective detection of 3NT, which can open a window for the synthesis of doped GQDs via 2D MXene materials for ultrasensitive and selective detection of other biometabolites and biomarkers of neurodegenerative diseases in biological fluids.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of Ti3C2Tx and NSGQDs. (a) TEM image of 2D Ti3C2Tx MXene (Inset: Tyndall effect) and (b) Ti3C2Tx-derived NSGQDs (inset: histogram of NSGQDs), (c) HRTEM images and Fast Fourier Transform pattern (inset: the lattice spacing of NSGQDs, (d) AFM image of NSGQDs, (e) TEM mapping and (f) EDS spectra of as prepared NSGQDs.

(a) The UV–visible spectra of NSGQDs, (b) the emission fluorescence spectra of NSGQDs at different excitation wavelengths of 200–260 nm (inset: photo of NSGQDs after irradiation of 365 nm UV light); and XRD patterns of (c) as-prepared N -doped MXene nanosheet and (d) Ti3C2Tx-derived NSGQDs.
The (a) FTIR and (b) Raman spectra of the as-prepared N-doped MXene nanosheet and NSGQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectra of N-doped MXene nanosheet and NSGQDs, and the deconvoluted XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s, and (e) Ti 2p of N-doped MXene nanosheet.
Deconvoluted XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) S 2p (inset: S 2s) of N–Ti3C2Tx-derived NSGQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of NSGQDs in the presence of 0–150 μM 3NT, (b) the change in fluorescence intensity (I/Io) after the addition of 0–150 μM 3NT, (c) the effect of pHs on the change in I/Io of NSGQDs in the presence of 150 μM 3NT, and (d) selectivity of NSGQDs toward 150 μM 3NT detection over 12 interferences.

head.ce:title: MXene nanosheet-derived N, S-codoped graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive and selective detection of 3-nitro- l -tyrosine in human serum

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 3-Nitro-l-tyrosine (3NT) is an oxidative stress metabolite associated with neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease and rheumatoid arthritis. In this study, the N, S-co-doped graphene quantum dots (NSGQDs) derived from nitrogen-doped Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheet via the hydrothermal method in the presence of mercaptosuccinic acid was synthesized as an optical sensing probe to detect 3NT in human serum. Tetramethyl ammonium hydroxide, the nitrogen source and delamination agent, was used to prepare nitrogen-doped MXene nanosheets via one step at room temperature. The as-prepared NSGQDs are uniform with an average size of 1.2 ± 0.6 nm, and can be stable in aqueous solution for at least 90 d to serve as the fluorescence probe. The N atoms in N-MXene reduce the restacking and aggregation of MXene nanosheets, while the sulfur dopant in NSGQDs increases the quantum yield from 6.2 to 12.1 % as well as enhances the selectivity of 3NT over the other 12 interferences via coordination interaction with nitro group in 3NT. A linear range of 0.02–150 μM in PBS and 0.05–200 μM in human serum with a recovery of 97–108 % for 3NT detection is observed. Moreover, the limit of detection can be lowered to 4.2 and 7 nM in PBS and 1 × diluted human serum, respectively. Results obtained clearly indicate the potential application of the N–Ti3C2Tx derived NSGQD for effective detection of 3NT, which can open a window for the synthesis of doped GQDs via 2D MXene materials for ultrasensitive and selective detection of other biometabolites and biomarkers of neurodegenerative diseases in biological fluids.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: 3-Nitro-l-tyrosine (3NT) is considered as a bioindicator of neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's diseases and rheumatoid arthritis resulting from the reaction of nitrogen species with either protein-free or protein-bound tyrosine residues [1]. Furthermore, 3NT is recognized as a biomarker for the diagnosis of pathological conditions induced by oxidative stress, which plays a pivotal role in sensing and therapy of pathogenesis [2]. Several studies have indicated that the 3NT concentration in the plasma of healthy persons is in the range of 4.54 ± 2.75 nM, and increases to 17.42 ± 11.6 nM and 14.8 ± 4.8 nM for healthy smokers and coronary artery disease patients, respectively [3]. Currently, several analytical techniques such as liquid chromatography, gas chromatography [4], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays have been employed to determine 3NT levels with high sensitivity [5]. However, these methods are relatively complicated, expensive, and time-consuming. Therefore, the searching of an easy and highly efficient sensing technique to rapid and ultrasensitive detection of 3NT for early diagnosis applications is quite urgent.

MXene, a 2-dimensional (2D) material first introduced in 2011, is comprised of transition metal carbide, nitride, and carbonitride. Generally, MXene materials are prepared by selectively etching of “A” layer from the 3D MAX phase, where A-layer mainly contains Al or Ti atoms [6]. This newly developed material not only has the similarity to graphene, but also has excellent structural properties such as good electrochemical stability and tunable functional groups [7] Ti3C2Tx MXene, the first developed MXene material [8], has received the most attention because of its distinguished merits such as good biocompatibility and ease of functionalization. Several studies have recently been successful fabricated MXene nanosheet and applied to a wide variety of fields including energy, environment, and sensing [9]. Interestingly, MXene nanosheet can be used to produce MXene quantum dots (MQDs) in which different sizes of MQDs can be tuned by controlling the synthesis temperature [10]. Since then, the emergence of nanosized Ti3C2Tx MQDs has raised a great deal of interest and opened a new window for versatile studies [11]. The conversion of 2D MXene nanosheets into 0D MQDs would lead to the prominent edges and quantum confinement effects. As a result, Ti3C2 QDs not only retain the inherent advantages of their 2D counterparts but also with improved performance such as enhanced solubility, feasible functionalization, and excellent luminescence [12]. However, MQDs contain metal ions inside the structure, leading to the low stability of sensing probe [13]. Therefore, the elimination of metal ions from MXene nanosheets to fabricate graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with excellent stability is a promising strategy to effectively detect 3NT.

The Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets have highly active chemical characteristics on the surface in comparison with carbon nanomaterials, which can provide abundant reactive sites for sensing as well as for modification [14]. Moreover, doping with organic and inorganic elements is considered as an effective strategy to enhance the properties of MXene-derived GQDs for various applications. Among heteroatoms used, nitrogen element can induce the graphitization by the bonding of Ti-Nx. Several studies have developed N-doped MXene for hydrogen evolution reaction, lithium battery, oxygen reduction reaction and electrochemical sensor [15,16]. In addition, doping with sulfur atoms have been widely accepted for increasing the interlayer spacing of carbon structure, quantum yields, and anchoring sites of carbon materials [17]. Although several studies have used Ti3C2Tx nanosheets as the precursor to fabricate GQDs for fluorescence ink and bioimaging purposes [21,22], the application of MXene-derived GQDs for sensing purposes, especially the metabolite detection, has rarely reported. It is noted that MXene-derived GQDs has a low cytotoxicity [14] and the irregular surface edges can not only provide hydrophilic functional groups but can also create more active sites for sensing purposes. This gives a great impetus to develop MXene-derived doped GQDs for the enhanced sensing performance of bioindicators in biological fluids.

In this study, for the first time, we have fabricated highly stable nitrogen and sulfur-codoped GQDs (NSGQDs) derived from nitrogen-doped MXene nanosheet, and then used as a fluorescence sensing probe for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of 3NT. As shown in Scheme 1 , Ti3C2Tx was prepared from Ti3AlC2 MAX phase by in-situ generated HF hydrothermal method. Nitrogen-doped MXene nanosheets was simply synthesized by using tetramethyl ammonium hydroxide (TMAOH) as a delamination and dopant agents via one step approach at room temperature, which can be stably dispersed in deionized (DI) water for 1 month. The NSGQDs were then synthesized at 180 °C for 12 h in the presence of N-MXene and mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA). The presence of sulfur dopant in GQDs enhances the sensitivity and selectivity of 3NT. Co-doping with N and S elements enhances the fluorescence quantum yield of MXene-derived NSGQDs from 6.2 to 12.1 %. The interaction of 3NT with NSGQDs results in the quench of the fluorescence intensity. A wide dynamic range of 0.02–150 μM 3NT with limit of detection (LOD) of 4.2 nM in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) is observed. The NSGQDs sensing probe also exhibit excellent analytical performance in biological fluids, and a linear range of 0.05–200 μM with a LOD of 7.0 nM and recovery of 98–108 % in diluted human serum is observed. Results of this study have opened a window to synthesize N–Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets and NSGQDs for direct detection of 3NT, which can be extended to other clinical trials for early detection and diagnosis of diseases.

The nitrogen-doped Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheet was fabricated by adding TMAOH as the delamination agent and N source. Briefly, 0.3 g of Ti3AlC2 powder was ground into small pieces and then immersed into 20 mL of solution containing 1.2 M LiF and 6 M HCl. The solution was heated at 60 °C for 24 h. The resultant product was then centrifuged at 3500 rpm with DI water repeatedly to separate the black powder of multilayer Ti3C2Tx MXene from liquid until the solution pH was close to 7, and then dried at 60 °C for 6 h in the vacuum oven. Finally, the appropriate amount of solid material was mixed with 25 % TMAOH at room temperature for 24 h for the delamination of the OH-terminated Ti3C2Tx MXene. Subsequently, the obtained N–Ti3C2Tx MXene was frozen by using free dryer for 24 h. Then, DI water was added to disperse N-MXene nanosheets and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation NSGQDs derived from N-MXene nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The NSGQDs derived from N-doped Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheet were prepared by using hydrothermal method, and further purified by 1 k Da dialysis bag for 24 h. In brief, 0.02 g N-doped MXene nanosheet was mixed with different weights (0.17, 0.27 and 0.37 mg) of MSA (Sigma-Aldrich) as the sulfur source. Then, the mixture was heated to 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to the room temperature, the mixture was purified by using 1 k Da dialysis bag to remove unreacted reagent with DI water as the dialysate until the pH reached to around 7. The final product was stored at room temperature. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of NSGQDs was checked every 24 h to confirm the stability of NSGQDs in solution. It is noted that the effect of sulfur content in NSGQDs on optical property was optimized, and an addition of 0.27 mg MSA was optimized because of its highest photoluminescence (PL) intensity (Fig. S1). This result is mainly attributed to the fact that the S dopant serves as an energy trap to enhance the light intensity at low concentration. However, high concentration of S dopant may have quenching effect to decrease the PL intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 3NT by N–Ti3C2Tx MXene derived NSGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of 3NT by MXene-derived NSGQDs was investigated at room temperature. Generally, 10 μL of NSGQDs in PBS at pH 7 were used for all the experiments. After the addition of 0–150 μM 3 NT for 1 min, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded, and the change in fluorescence intensity (I/I0) at 345 nm, where I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensities of NSGQDs before and after the addition of 3NT, was calculated to determine the calibration curve. Each experiment was triplicated to calculate the relative standard error (RSD). Moreover, the change in I/I0 at different pHs of 5–9 in the presence of 150 μM 3NT was also examined to understand the impact of pH on sensing system. The stability of NSGQDs was investigated under different conditions including temperature, photoirradiation, and co-ions. A 4-W of 365-nm ultraviolet lamp (BLAK RAY, USA) was used to test the photostability of the NSGQDs. For co-ion effect, 1 mL of ion solutions (Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+, and NH4 +) was added into 1 mL of NSGQD-containing solution to get a final concentration of 0.15 mM. The temperature effect on the stability of NSQGD was conducted at different temperatures ranging from 45 to 80 °C. The fluorescence intensity of all the mixtures was determined after well-stirring for 3 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of N–Ti3C2Tx derived NSGQDs in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the flexibility of using N–Ti3C2Tx derived NSGQDs as the sensing probe to detect 3NT in biological fluids, the diluted human serum sample was used as the matrix for real applications. Human serum from GemCell™ (Table S1) was first centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove the impurities. The obtained supernatant was then diluted with 0.1 M PBS at the human serum-to-PBS ratios of 1:1 and 2:1. All the experiments were run triplicate to make sure the QA/QC of analytical procedures. Fig. S2 shows the fluorescence intensity of NSGQDs after the addition of 3NT in different dilution ratios of human serum at pH 7. It is clear that the fluorescence intensity of diluted human serum at a ratio of 1:1 is 3 times higher than that of control. The increase in human-to-PBS ratio to 2:1 decreases the fluorescence intensity because of the matrix effect, elaborating the optimal dilution ratio of 1 × for further experiments. The performance of the sensing probe was examined by spiking various concentrations of 3NT in human serum. Concentrations of 3NT in samples were in the range of 50 nM–200 μM. All the assays were repeated three times, and the analytical performance were measured by the change in I/Io. Furthermore, the human serum testing was carried out by following the National Tsing Hua University's IRB and ethical criteria (IRB No. 10802HM004).

The structural properties and particle sizes of N–Ti3C2Tx derived NSGQDs were identified with a JEOL transmission electron microscope (TEM) and a JEOL high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM). The Agilent 5500 scanning probe microscope in tapping mode was used to investigate the change in topography as well as to calculate the thickness of NSGQDs. The Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer was employed to identify the crystallinity of NSGQDs. A Horiba FT720 Infrared spectrometer was employed to measure the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the sensing probe. The chemical species of N-MXene derived NSGQDs was obtained by an ESCA Ulvac-PHI 1600 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) at 1486.6 ± 0.2 eV. The fluorescence and UV–visible spectra were carried out by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence and a U-4100 UV–visible spectrophotometers, respectively. Raman spectra of N-MXene nanosheets and NSGQDs were obtained by a Bruker Senterra micro-Raman spectrometer.

The morphology of MAX phase-derived N-MXene nanosheets was first identified by SEM images. Fig. S3a shows the morphology of 3D integrated structure of MAX phase. After etching with in-situ generated HF, the 2D MXene nanosheet is clearly observed (Fig. S3b). Addition of TMAOH is conducive to prepare the delaminated N-MXene. As illustrated in Fig. S3c, few-layered N-MXene structure is obtained by using TMAOH as the delamination agent and N source. These SEM images clearly indicate the successful fabrication of the delaminated 2D N-MXene from 3D MAX phase.

The TEM images were used to confirm the structure of the delaminated N-MXene. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the clean and smooth surface of pristine few-layered N–Ti3C2Tx nanosheets with a diameter of 0.5–1 μm is obtained after the treatment of TMAOH. Moreover, the exfoliated Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets shows long-term stable dispersion in water due to the abundant –OH functional groups, which is in good agreement with the previous report [18]. When irradiating with the green laser light at 532 nm, the Tyndall effect of MXene nanosheets is clearly observed (inset of Fig. 1a), which is consistent with the previous report [19]. After the hydrothermal treatment, the TEM image shows the monodispersed and well crystalline N–Ti3C2-derived NSGQDs particles (Fig. 1b). The particle size is in the range of 1.1–1.6 nm with an average size of 1.2 ± 0.6 nm (Inset of Fig. 1b). In addition, the quantum yield, using fluorescein sodium as a standard reference (Φ = 0.79) [20], is calculated as 6.2 % for N-MXene-derived NGQDs and 12.1 % for N-MXene-derived NSGQDs, which is comparable to and even higher than those reported MXene-derived GQDs [21,22]. The high quantum yield is mainly attributed to the fact that doping with sulfur atoms in the backbone of graphene structure of GQDs can generate the intrinsic density of NSGQD, resulting in the enhancement of quantum yield of NSGQDs [23].

The crystal structure of the NSGQD was analyzed by HR-TEM, and the clear fringes of carbon lattices in the NSGQDs is observed (Fig. 1c). The distance between adjacent lattice fringes is 0.232 nm, which can be assigned as the (100) plane of GQDs [24]. The SAED pattern of NSGQDs in the inset of Fig. 1c shows the 6-circle ring-like structure, clearly indicating the high crystallinity of NSGQDs. Fig. 1d depicts the AFM image of the thickness distribution of NSGQDs. The thickness is in the range of 0–0.8 nm, which means that the N–Ti3C2Tx-derived NSGQDS only contain 2–4 layers, which is in good agreement with a previous report [24]. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopic (EDS) analysis (Fig. 1e and f) further provides evidence on the distribution of elemental composition. In addition to C and O elements, a high content of sulfur at 13.4 at% and a certain level of nitrogen (3.7 at%) are observed, showing that S and N atoms are successfully doped into the NSGQD structure. It is noteworthy the no Ti and Al elements are found in the EDS spectra, indicating that Al and Ti atoms are completely removed when converting 2D N-MXene nanosheet structure into 0D quantum dots [25].

Fig. S4a and Fig. 2a show the UV–visible absorption spectra of the NGQDs and NSGQDs derived from N-doped MXene nanosheet at 200–800 nm, respectively. Clearly, NGQDs exhibits an absorbance peak at 270 nm (Fig. S4a). Addition of sulfur dopant extends the adsorption to long wavelength, and NSGQDs exhibit a distinct absorption band at 310 nm, which can be assigned as the absorption band of n–π*. In addition, the absorption spectrum extends its tail to the region of 220–270 nm, which is the electron transit from the π–π* of graphitic sp2 [26]. The fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared NSGQDs after excitation with different wavelengths of 200–260 nm is shown in Fig. 2 b. The emission peak position appears at 345 nm and is independent on the excitation wavelength, indicating the stable fluorescence property of NSGQDs. Moreover, the emission of NSGQDs exhibits a violet fluorescence color because of their ultra-small size effect (inset of Fig. 2b) [27]. The excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of the undoped N-GQDs show that the fluorescence intensity increases as the excitation wavelength increases from 280 to 310 nm (Fig. S4b). Again, the intensity decreases obviously in the excitation wavelength range of 320–350 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence peak shifts from 425 to 450 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 280 to 350 nm. This result clearly indicates that the doping with S atoms changes the optical property of N-MXene derived GQDs, which is more suitable and stable as the sensing probe for 3NT detection.

The XRD patterns of MAX phase, N-MXene and N-MXene-derived NSGQDs were recorded. As depicted in Fig. S5, the pristine MAX phase exhibits peaks at 2θ of 10.06, 19.7, 34.5, 37.3, 39.5, 42.4, 49.04, and 56.9⁰, corresponding to the diffraction peaks of (002), (004), (101), (103), (104), (105), (107), and (109) planes of Ti3AlC2. Interestingly, the peak of (104), representing the “Al-layer” in the structure of Ti3AlC2, disappears in the MXene nanosheets (Fig. 2c). Moreover, the (002) peak shifts from 10.06° 2θ in MAX phase to 6.4° 2θ in MXene, and the d-spacing changes from 5.4 to 13.03 Å. The change in the peak intensity of (002) plane corroborates the successful synthesis of N-doped MXene nanosheet. In addition, the XRD patterns of NSGQDs (Fig. 2d) shows a broad peak at 21° 2θ, confirming the presence of (002) plane of graphite [28,29].

FTIR spectra of N-doped MXene nanosheet and N-MXene-derived NSGQDs is shown in Fig. 3 a. The N–Ti3C2Tx shows peaks at 3443, 3153, 2000, 1643, 895, 768, 628, and 589 cm−1, which represent the existence of hydrophilic –OH, N–H, C–N, CO, C–F and Ti–O bonds [30,31]. A broad peak located at 3442 cm−1 elaborates that the surface terminated group of N-MXene is –OH group [32]. After hydrothermal treatment with N-MXene in the presence of MSA, the FTIR spectrum of NSGQDs still shows a clear peak at 3442 cm−1, which indicates the hydrophilic property of NSGQDs. A strong absorption band at 2923 cm−1 is the C–H bond, and the peak at 2860 cm−1 can be ascribed to the S–H binding. Furthermore, peaks at 1637 and 1387 cm−1 are related to the C–N and SO bonds of graphene, the backbone of NSGQDs. The peak in the band of 1500–1700 cm−1 can be assigned to the CC bond [33]. Peak at 1085 and 620 cm−1 belong to the C–S bond, and the band at 796 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of C–N [34,35]. The results indicate that sulfur and nitrogen present in the edge sites of GQDs. Moreover, the vibration at 603 cm−1 represents the C–F bonding.

The Raman spectra of the as-synthesized delaminated N-MXene nanosheet and NSGQDs is shown in Fig. 3b. The characteristic peaks at 1354 (D band) and 1595 (G band) cm−1 are observed (inset of Fig. 3b), which are from the defect site in the disordered carbon and ordered sp2 carbon, respectively [29,35]. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of NSGQDs is 0.96, indicating the amorphous nature of carbon. Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum shows the quantum confinement effect of NSGQDs. As shown in Fig. S6, the photoluminescence intensity changes when sulfur atoms are doped into the N-MXene structure. The blue shift in the PL indicates the reduction of GQDs size after doping with S, depicting the formation of defect in GQDs due to the contribution of S as a donor atom [36].

To elucidate the chemical compositions and valence states of the as-prepared sensing probe, XPS analysis of N-MXene and N–Ti3C2Tx derived NSGQDs was conducted. The XPS full survey spectrum of Ti3AlC2 MAX phase in Fig. S7a shows several peaks located at 531, 457, 284, and 72.6 eV, which belong to the O 1s, Ti 2p, C 1s and Al 2p. After the hydrothermal treatment with TMAOH, the full survey spectrum of N-doped MXene exhibits peaks at 458.9 (Ti 2p), 531.1 (O 1s), 285.1 (C 1s), and 401.2 eV (N 1s), which are the basal elements of N–Ti3C2Tx (T: OH group) (Fig. 4 a). Besides, a small peak at 70.9 eV which can be assigned to Al 2p is also observed. This means that N-MXene nanosheet still contains trace amount of Al atoms. Since no Al is observed in EDS mapping and XRD patterns, the existence of a trace amount of Al is most likely from the deposition of Al residue of the etching solution. Moreover, the existence of F 1s peak at 686 eV is attributed to the fact that the Ti3C2 nanosheets are synthesized by the in-situ generated HF etching method in the presence of LiF and HCl.

The XPS full survey spectrum of NSGQDs presents five main peaks at 284, 531, 400, 232 and 164 eV, which correspond to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, S 2s and S 2p, respectively (Fig. 4a), highlighting the presence of N and S elements in the GQDs structures. Interestingly, a blue shift in binding energies of C1s, N 1s, and O 1s in the survey spectra of NSGQDs in comparison with N-MXene is observed, which means that the charge redistribution of NSGQDs occurs during the derivation from N–Ti3C2Tx [37]. It is noteworthy that the atomic percentages of C, O, N, and S of N-MXene-derived NSGQDs, calculated from the XPS full survey spectrum, are 50.74, 39.45, 5.52 and 3.29 %, respectively. The C-to-O ratio is in good agreement with those reported studies [28,36,38], elaborating the successful fabrication of NSGQDs in this study.

The deconvoluted XPS C1s spectrum of MAX phase in Fig. S7b shows peaks at 280.6 (Ti–C), 284.2(C–C), and 287.8 (C–O) eV. The C–O bond is derived from the oxidation of MXene and the adsorption of oxygen on the surface of MAX phase. The deconvoluted Al 2p of MAX phase shows three main peaks at 71.2, 73.4, and 74.2 eV (Fig. S7c), which are associated with Al, Al–O and Al–C, respectively [38,39]. The deconvoluted Ti2p peak in Ti3AlC2 presents several peaks at 453.3, 454.2, 457.5, 459.8 and 463.3 eV, which are mainly from the bonding of Ti–Al, TiO2, and Ti–C, respectively (Fig. S7d).

Similar to the MAX phase, the deconvoluted XPS peak of C 1s in N–Ti3C2Tx still shows three deconvoluted peaks at 281.5 (Ti–C), 284.8 (C–C) and 288.8 (C–O) eV (Fig. 4b). Two peaks at 400.4 and 395.9 eV are observed in the deconvoluted N1s spectra, indicating the formation of CN and Ti–N bonds, respectively (Fig. 4c). It is noted that the NC signal is the evidence of successful doping of nitrogen atoms in the layer of carbon during the synthesis process. The O 1s spectrum of N–Ti3C2Tx MXene can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 530.3 and 532 eV, which belong to the TiO2 and C–Ti-Ox or C–Ti–OH groups, respectively (Fig. 4d). Likewise, the high proportion of TiO2 component is clearly observed in the deconvoluted Ti 2p spectrum at 458.7 eV (Fig. 4e), which elaborates the oxidation of Ti on the surface of MXene. Besides, the deconvoluted peaks at 454.1, 455 and 456.5 eV in the Ti 2p spectrum are the characteristic peaks of Ti–C, Ti–N, and Ti–O bonding, respectively. Moreover, the 2 deconvoluted peaks at 684.3 and 686.5 eV in the F 1s peak of the N-MXene are F–Ti and F–C groups, respectively, which is attributed to the etching process of using in-situ generated HF convert MAX phase into N-MXene nanosheet (Fig. S8).

The XPS full survey of the as-prepared NSGQDs presents the basal C, O, N, S peaks. According to Fig. 5 a, the deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of NSGQDs consists of four peaks of C–C/CC (284 eV) [40], C–N/C–S (284.3 eV), C–O (285.6eV) and CO (288.0 eV) groups, confirming the interaction of graphitic structures of GQDs with N and S atoms as well as the presence of carbonyl and carboxylic functional groups on the surface or at the edge. In addition, the O 1s exhibits three peaks centering at 530.5, 531.4, and 532.8 eV, which are the C–O, O–H and O–S groups, respectively (Fig. 5b). Similarly, the binding energies of N 1s at 399.6 and 401.1 eV indicates the pyrrolic nitrogen contribution from C–N–C and N–H, respectively (Fig. 5c) [41]. Similarly, peaks at 164.2, 167.9, 168.9, and 231 eV in the XPS S 2p and S2s spectra can be assigned as the thiophene (2p3/2 and 2p1/2), sulfone, and thiosulfonate (Fig. 5d) [42–44], highlighting the successful doping of heteroatoms (nitrogen and sulfur) into the graphitic surface.

The optical sensing of 3NT using N-MXene-derived NSGQDs as the probe was carried out. Fig. 6 a shows that the fluorescence intensity of NSGQDs at 345 nm is quenched linearly after the addition of different concentrations of 3NT in the range of 0–150 μM. Fig. 6b illustrates the change in I/I0 as a function of 3NT concentration. A good linear relationship between I/I0 and 3NT concentration in the range of 30 nM–150 μM with a correlation coefficient (r2) of 0.986 is obtained. The LOD of 3NT by N-MXene-derived NSGQDs, calculated from the 3δ/S where δ represents the relative standard deviation from 7 replicate analyses, and S represents 3NT concentration used for analysis [20], is 4.2 nM. The excellent linear range and low LOD is attributed to the doping of sulfur heteroatoms to enhance the defects on the surface of GQDs, especially the cluster of carbon (sp2) [42], resulting in the enhanced quantum yield of NSGQDs as well as the improved sensitivity of 3NT detection. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity of N-doped GQDs reduces 50 % when 150 μM of 3NT is added to the solution. However, a 90 % reduction of the I/Io is obtained when N, S-codoped GQDs are used as the sensing probe, corroborating the superiority of using NSGQDs are the fluorescence probe for ultrasensitive detection of 3NT.

The sensing performance of 3NT at different pH values was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 6c, the change in I/Io of NSGQDs increases with an increase in pH values from 5 to 9, primarily due to the deprotonation of carboxylic acid group at high pH values. The dramatic decrease in I/Io after the addition of 150 μM 3NT at pH 5–9 indicates that the NSGQDs can serve as an excellent fluorescent sensor for the detection of 3NT. Additionally, Fig. S10 shows the response time of NSGQDs in the presence of 150 μM 3NT. The I/Io of NSGQDs decreases rapidly within 1 min and then remains unchanged after 120 min, confirming the short response time and irreversible reaction of N-MXene derived NSGQD toward 3NT detection. Since pH 7 is most effective on fluorescence quenching in the presence of 3NT, pH 7 and 1 min of reaction time are, therefore, selected for the further experiments.

The selectivity is one of the huge challenges in sensing platforms. In this study, selectivity study toward 3NT detection was examined using various common analytes including cyanuric acid, glutathione (GSH), acetic acid (AA), glycine, glucose, acetylsalicylic, l-cystine, creatine, uric acid, l-glutamine, trypsin, and l-cysteine as the interferers. Fig. 6d shows that the I/Io of NSGQDs has no obvious change in the presence of 200 μM various interferers, while the I/Io decreases remarkably after addition of 3NT. Interestingly, a significant change in I/Io is obtained when all the interferers are mixed with 3NT and the intensity ratio in NSGQDs is almost similar to that of individual 3NT, which confirms the high selectivity of NSGQDs toward 3NT detection.

The stability of the as-prepared NSGQDs under different conditions including temperature, photo-irradiation, and co-ions was further examined. Results show that the N-MXene-derived NSGQDs are stable under different operational conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S11a, NSGQDs are stable and can maintain the fluorescence intensity for at least 90 d at 25 °C, which corroborates the excellent long-term stability of N-MXene derived NSGQDs at room temperature. Besides, the fluorescence intensity (I/Io) ranges from 0.83 to 0.99 after the addition of 0.15 mM metal ions including Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+, and NH4 + (Fig. S11b), depicting that the existence of co-ions has little influence on the stability of NSGQDs. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of NSGQDs decreases slightly after the irradiation of 365-nm UV light for 90 min (Fig. S11c). In addition, the effect of temperature on the stability of NSGQDs was examines, and the change in temperature from room temperature to 80 °C has little impact on the fluorescence intensity of NSGQDs (Fig. S11d). Moreover, the stability of MXene-derived NSQGDs in 1 × diluted human serum was also investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S12a, the fluorescence intensity only decreases by 2–3 % after the exposure to a UV lamp (365 nm, 8 W) for 90 min. Only a slight change in fluorescence intensity of NSGQDs is observed in the temperature range of 45–80 °C (Fig. S12b). These results clearly indicate that the N-MXene-derived NSGQDs are quite stable under different conditions, elaborating the excellent optical properties of NSGQDs for serving as the sensing probe to detect 3NT in aqueous solutions.

To further clarify the sensing mechanism of NSGQDs for 3NT detection, static and dynamic quenching phenomena in the presence of various concentrations of 3NT were examined. In contrast, dynamic quenching occurs when the excited fluorescent probe and the quencher collide in a diffusion-controlled manner. Usually, the static quenching phenomenon of an analytes can be explained by the fluorescence spectra after the addition of various concentrations of analyte, which can serve as the quencher to decrease the fluorescence intensity by the creation of a non-luminescent or less fluorescent complex (Fig. 6a). NSGQDs have the potential to interact with 3NT through π-π stacking, electrostatic interaction, and chemical bonding. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of NSGQDs decreases dramatically upon increasing the concentration of 3NT, which indicates the successful binding of 3NT on the surface of NSGQD through π-π stacking of the carboxylic (−COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups for static quenching [45].

The fluorescence lifetime is a good indicator to assess the quenching mechanism. The fluorescence lifetime remains unchanged for the static quenching, while the dynamic quenching induces the change in the fluorescence lifetime in the presence of a quencher. To further understand the possible sensing mechanism, the measurement of time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of NSGQDs and the calculation of the Stern-Volmer plot in the presence of 3NT were employed. Fig. 7 a displays the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of NSGQDs in the presence of various concentrations of 3NT in a PBS solution at pH 7. The relaxation time of NSGQDs decreases from 7.8 ns in the absence of 3NT to 6.9 ns at 150 μM 3NT, clearly indicating that the presence of 3NT significantly shortens the relaxation time of NSGQDs.

Fig. 7b shows the Stern-Volmer plot of NSGQDs after the interaction with 3NT. A linear relationship between τ/τo, where τ and τo represent the average fluorescence lifetime in the presence and absence of 3NT, respectively, and the concentration of 3NT was observed. The observed linearity indicates a systematic and predictable dynamic quenching behavior with respect to the concentration of 3NT. Typically, the dynamic quenching takes place via either Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or electron transfer (ET) [46]. The high selectivity of NS-GQDs for 3NT detection is primarily attributed to the strong coordination reaction between sulfur atoms in the graphitic carbon backbone and the nitro functional group of 3NT. This interaction results in a high specificity for 3NT detection [47]. Therefore, the sulfur dopant contributes significantly to the quench of fluorescence intensity in this study.

The zeta potential of NSGQDs was measured before and after the addition of 3NT at pH 7. As depicted in Fig. S13, the negatively charged surface of NSGQDs lowers the zeta potential to – 8.9 mV originally. Moreover, the zeta potential decreases to – 13.3 mV after the addition of 3NT, presumably attributed to the electrostatic interaction between NSGQDs and 3NT. In addition, the XPS N 1s spectra of NSGQDs were compared before and after the introduction of 3NT (Fig. S14). The N 1s spectrum of NSGQDs in the absence of 3NT displays a broad peak at 400 eV with two distinct peaks at 399.6 and 401.1 eV, corresponding to the N element in the pyridine group and coordinated –NH2 group, respectively. After the addition of 3NT, all peaks exhibit a slight redshift, elaborating the interaction of 3NT with the surface of NSGQDs.

Scheme 2 illustrates the possible mechanism of NSGQDs for the detection of 3NT. To better understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the UV–visible absorption spectra of NSGQDs in the presence of various concentrations of 3NT was further recorded. As depicted in Fig. S15, the position of UV–visible peaks remains unchanged after adding 1–150 μM 3NT to the NSGQDs solution, confirming the dynamic nature of the quenching process. Additionally, the absorption spectra of 3 μM 3NT was also determined in the absence of NSGQDs (inset of Fig. S15a). The low absorption peak of 3NT indicates that peaks at 345 nm is mainly attributed to the interaction between NSGQDs and 3NT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 3NT by N–Ti3C2Tx derived NSGQDs in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the applicability of the N-MXene-derived NQGDS probe for a clinical application, the detection of 3NT over NSGQDs was examined using diluted (1 × ) human serum as the matrix. Fig. 8 a displays the change in I/Io of N-MXene derived NSGQDs in the presence of 0–200 μM 3NT after excitation with 220 nm UV light. Similar to the fluorescence spectra of NSGQDs in PBS solution, the fluorescence intensity at 345 nm deceases upon increasing 3NT concentration in the 1 × diluted human serum. It is noteworthy that the unsmoothing fluorescence curves in Fig. 8a are mainly attributed to the interference of human serum matrix. The blue shift in fluorescence peak after adding 3NT can be explained by the coordination interaction between NSQGDs and 3NT in human serum. The presence of 3NT in human serum can induce changes in the electronic structure of NSGQDs because of the quantum confinement effect, and subsequently results in a blue shift in optical properties. Fig. 8b illustrates the linear relationship between I/Io and 3NT concentration of 50 nM–200 μM. A dynamic range of 5 orders of magnitude with r2 of 0.984 in human serum is obtained, which is comparable to the analytical performance of 3NT in PBS. Moreover, the LOD is determined to be 7.0 nM, which indicates the little matrix effect on the 3NT detection in human serum.

Table 1 compares the analytical performance of 3NT detection using different inorganic and organic sensing probes. The LODs of 3NT detected by other reported sensing probes like molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) and CdWO4@rGO are in the range of 3.24 nM–30 μM [5,48–52]. A previous study used ELISA-based sandwich method to detect 3NT, and found that the 3NT concentrations in cerebrospinal fluid were in the range of 928 ± 245 nM [53]. Furthermore, a sandwich ELISA is applied to detect 3NT in the serum of systemic lupus erythematosus patients, and the concentration range of 96.5 ± 21.1 μM was obtained [54]. In this work, a highly sensitive detection of 3NT with dynamic range of 4–5 orders of magnitude in both PBS and human serum samples is obtained when the N-MXene-derived NSGQDs are used as the sensing probe. It is noted that the no human serum from patients was tested in this study because of the limitation of IRB and ethical criteria of hospitals. However, the analytical performance of N-MXene-derive NSGQDs can cover the concentration range from healthy persons to patients. Moreover, the LOD can be lower to 4.2–7.0 nM, elaborating the superiority of the developed NSGQDs for 3NT detection in human serum. Interestingly, the LODs of NSGQDs is comparable with or even superior to the sensors shown in Table 1, indicating that N-MXene derived NSGQD is a promising material for rapid and sensitive detection of bioindicators in human fluids.

Precision is also key factor to influence the development of sensing technique for clinical application. Therefore, different 3NT concentrations were spiked into 1 × diluted human serum for recovery experiments. Every spiked concentration in human serum was repeated for at least 3 times to calculate the precision. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of 3NT in human serum by using the standard addition method are in the range of 97–108 % after spiking 4 different levels of 3NT ranging from 70 nM to 150 μM. In addition, the relative standard deviations of all the spiked concentrations are in the range of 3.5–5.9 %, which is comparable to that in PBS (106 ± 3.8 %), clearly highlighting that the developed sensing technique is highly reliable for optical detection of 3NT by using N–Ti3C2Tx-derived NSGQDs as the fluorescence probe.

In this study, the few-layer N–Ti3C2Tx-derived NSGQDs have been successfully fabricated as the fluorescence probe for ultrasensitive and selective detection of 3NT in both PBS and diluted human serum. The use of TMAOH as the delamination and N-doping agent can fabricate 2–4 layers of N-MXene, making the 1.2 ± 0.6 nm NSGQD homogeneous and well-dispersed in solution. Moreover, the doping with heteroatoms of sulfur element in carbon backbone of GQDs not only enhances the quantum yield of NSGQDs from 6.2 % to 12.1 % but can also undergo the coordination reaction with nitro group in 3NT for effective detection of 3NT. The NSGQDs exhibit an excellent analytical performance on 3NT detection, and a dynamic range of 4–5 orders of magnitude of 3NT in both PBS and 1 × human serum is obtained. Furthermore, the LOD of 3NT is as low as 4.2 nM in PBS and 7.0 nM in human serum, indicating the superiority of the N–Ti3C2Tx derived NSGQDs toward 3NT detection. The sensing probe also exhibits good selectivity over the other 12 interferences, and the recovery of 98–108 % in 1 × human serum is achieved. Additionally, the NSGQDs is stable and can maintain the morphology as well as optical property for at least 3 months. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time to fabricate N–Ti3C2Tx derived NSGQDs for the detection of 3NT, which can open a gateway to synthesize nitrogen-doped MXene nanosheets and NSGQDs for ultrasensitive and selective detection of biometabolites and biomarkers of neurodegenerative and other diseases clinically.

Nguyen Thi Ngoc Anh: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Trung Viet Huynh: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Van Thanh Nguyen: Visualization, Software, Investigation, Data curation. Thi Kim Anh Nguyen: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ruey-an Doong: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
